overview: Tron is a classic Pauper archetype that evolved from using Locus lands to relying on Urza's trio for massive mana acceleration. Historically a control toolbox deck, it has adapted through various iterations like Fog Tron, Egg Tron, Altar Tron, and Flicker Tron to survive bans and meta shifts. Modern versions blend consistent mana fixing with combo engines or resilient control strategies to dominate the format.
win_conditions: Return opponent's permanents to hand and force discards using Dinrova Horror
Deal lethal damage directly with Rolling Thunder
Generate infinite 3/3 tokens with Golem Foundry and attack for lethal
Create an infinite Golem army via Ashnod's Altar and Myr Retriever recursion

keep_criteria: Hands containing multiple Urza's lands or mana accelerants like Crop Rotation
Hands with early interaction, life gain, or card draw engines
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably Urza’s Mine/Power Plant/Tower), one mana accelerator (Sol Ring/Arcane Signet), and at least one early interaction or discard.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking land drops or mana sources
Hands with only high-cost finishers and no ramp or protection
Mulligan on 3+ lands with no Urzas, or hands with only accelerators and no lands.

examples.reason: Provides fast mana assembly, early board control, and a clear win condition path.

early_game: Prioritize casting Urza's trio or mana accelerants to unlock five-color mana
Play cheap interaction and life gain spells to stall the opponent and survive early pressure
Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, or use discard on a key threat.
Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another accelerator or discard, and begin sequencing Urza lands into your mana base.
Prioritize untapping lands that enable Tron by turn 3.
mid_game: Assemble the full mana base to access expensive answers and toolbox cards
Set up mini loops or combo pieces to lock down the opponent's resources and board state
Hoard mana until you can untap three Urza lands simultaneously.
Use Relic of Progenitus to protect your graveyard from recursion or combo engines.
Use Lembas sparingly for life or to enable Tron if mana is tight.
Commit threats only when you can protect them or when you have a clear path to victory.
Hold back Emrakul if the board is too unstable
use Koth to clear lands or Ugin to reset if needed.
late_game: Execute win conditions like infinite token generation, direct damage, or Golem armies
Maintain board control while cycling through the deck to find missing combo components
Emrakul, the Promised End (draws cards, bounces threats, wins via card advantage), Ugin, the Spirit Dragon (board wipe + card draw), or Koth, the Warhammer (destroys lands, pumps, and attacks).
Go all-in when you have assembled Tron, have answered or outpaced the opponent’s interaction, and can safely untap your threat.
If you have Emrakul and they have no removal, cast it immediately.

key_cards.name: Urza's trio
key_cards.notes: The core engine that provides massive mana to play expensive spells across all five colors.

key_cards.name: Bonder's Ornament
key_cards.notes: Fixes the mana curve and acts as a card draw engine similar to Monarch decks.

key_cards.name: Crop Rotation
key_cards.notes: Adopted to quickly find Urza's lands after Expedition Map was banned.

key_cards.name: Golem Foundry
key_cards.notes: Generates infinite 3/3 tokens or counters when paired with recursive loops.

key_cards.name: Ashnod's Altar
key_cards.notes: Enables infinite loops by sacrificing Myr to create mana and retrieve them from the graveyard.

sequencing_tips: Cast Urza's lands as early as possible to unlock five-color mana
Hold back combo pieces until the mana base is fully assembled
Sacrifice artifacts in the correct order to trigger recursive loops without stalling
Use life gain and combat control spells early to buy time for the combo
Candy Trail is not a recognized Magic card
focus on standard Tron staples like Urza’s lands, Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, and Koth/Emrakul/Ugin.
Common mistake: untapping Urza lands individually instead of saving them for a simultaneous Tron activation.
Always count your lands and accelerators to guarantee a turn 3 Tron.

matchups.advice: Prioritize early interaction and life gain to survive fast combo attempts
Use board wipes and artifact destruction to disrupt their token generation
Use Koth to destroy lands and break their mana, or cast Emrakul/Ugin when they’re tapped out. Use discard to remove counterspells before you cast your threats.
Out-ramp them with accelerators, use Relic to stop their graveyard ramp, and pressure with Koth or early discard to disrupt their land drops.
Use discard to remove key threats, hold up interaction for turn 2-3, and stabilize with Koth or early Emrakul if possible. Use Lembas for life to survive until your big threats.
Apply pressure with discard and Koth to destroy their lands. Use Emrakul to bypass counterspells and generate card advantage. Don’t overcommit to one threat; have backup plans.
Win by out-resourcing with Ugin or Emrakul, using Relic to deny their graveyard value, and leveraging discard to strip their key spells. Prioritize card advantage and graveyard hate.

common_threats: Watch for graveyard recursion (Elves, Jund), artifact chains (Affinity), and counterspells (Blue Terror, Grixis).
Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for interaction.

overview_evidence.evidence_span: Fog Tron, as it was known, was basically a control list that leaned on getting Urza's trio as fast as possible so you could take advantage of the extra mana they created to play expensive spells more easily.

win_conditions_evidence.evidence_span: Some lists used Dinrova Horror to return all the opponent's permanents to their hand and then force them to discard them.
Others used Rolling Thunder to win the game by dealing lethal damage directly.
With this loop and a Golem Foundry in play, you'll have an infinite number of 3/3 tokens and will be able to attack for lethal.
Finally, you'll put an infinite number of counters on Foundry to create a Golem army.

sequencing_tips_evidence.evidence_span: leaned on getting Urza's trio as fast as possible so you could take advantage of the extra mana they created
stalled the game until you could set up mini loops

If you sacrifice Myr with the Altar, you'll create and will be able to get another Myr from the graveyard. Then, you'll use the mana you created to put it in play again and repeat this process infinitely.

common_threats_evidence.evidence_span: Storm and Affinity dominated the format and were too much for old Tron, which disappeared quickly.
